title: Categorification and Geometry
speaker: Lars Hesselholt
abstract: The key principle in Grothendieck's algebraic geometry is that every commutative ring be considered as the ring of functions on some geometric object. Clausen and Scholze have introduced a categorification of algebraic and analytic geometry, where the key principle is that every stable closed symmetric monoidal infinity-category be considered as the infinity-category of quasi-coherent modules on some geometric object.
tags: Special Year Workshop on p-adic Arithmetic Geometry

proof_stages.label: Evenness as a geometric property
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by framing derived or graded-commutative geometry with signs and defining an 'even' map of rings as a Grothendieck-style geometric property stable under base change and local on source and target.

proof_stages.label: Localizing invariants and noncommutative motives
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the symmetric monoidal infinity category of dualizable stable presentable infinity categories, defines localizing invariants by preservation of zero objects, bicartesian squares, and filtered colimits, and recalls the universal localizing invariant into noncommutative motives.

proof_stages.label: THH as a symmetric monoidal localizing invariant
proof_stages.summary: For a dualizable stable infinity category, THH is constructed as the trace/evaluation-coevaluation composite on spectra, giving a symmetric monoidal localizing invariant valued in spectra with U(1)-action.

proof_stages.label: Idempotents, commutative algebras, and open-closed geometry
proof_stages.summary: Commutative geometry is organized through idempotent E0-algebras, which uniquely promote to idempotent commutative algebras, and through Clausen-Scholze style spectra that produce open/closed decompositions and bicartesian squares.

proof_stages.label: The even filtration on U(1)-Tate spectra
proof_stages.summary: After p-completing, the speaker defines even commutative algebras in spectra with U(1)-action and defines the even filtration on the Tate spectrum of an even object, extending it to arbitrary inputs by right Kan extension.

proof_stages.label: Evenly faithfully flat descent
proof_stages.summary: Using Hahn-Wilson's notion of an evenly faithfully flat map with even target, the even filtration of A^{tU(1)} is shown to be computable from the Cech complex of an evenly faithfully flat cover A -> B.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with BMS and Liu-Wang filtrations
proof_stages.summary: The descent theorem is applied to quasisyntomic rings via quasi-regular semiperfectoid covers and to a relative complete discrete valuation setting, identifying the even filtration with the Bhatt-Morrow-Scholze and Liu-Wang filtrations.

proof_stages.label: Stack spectral sequence, MU descent, and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The associated spectral sequence is described in terms of a graded/derived stack such as prismatization, and further consequences are drawn from the evenly faithfully flat map THH(MU) -> MU, including MU-relative resolutions and open questions about TC, cyclotomic filtrations, and categorical algebraic closures.

examples.name: Spectra as an idempotent algebra in Pr^L
examples.note: For C = Pr^L, the suspension-spectrum functor from anima/spaces to spectra is an idempotent E0-algebra, making spectra a commutative algebra whose modules are stable presentable infinity categories; this makes the idempotent-promotion principle concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the idempotent-algebra discussion to Pr^L, identifies the tensor unit and the Sigma-infinity-plus map to spectra, and traces consequences such as spectra becoming a commutative algebra and its modules being stable presentable categories.

passes.reason: Sp is idempotent in Pr^L, with tensor powers collapsing as a localization algebra, and is used illustratively.

Spectra in Pr^L is used as an idempotent/localizing algebra object, whose multiplication is equivalence-like rather than typical, not as a refutation.
As a commutative algebra object in Pr^L, spectra is idempotent with multiplication effectively collapsing to an equivalence, and it is used constructively.
As an idempotent algebra object, Sp has multiplication collapsing Sp⊗Sp back to Sp, and it is used only illustratively.
As an idempotent algebra in Pr^L, spectra has multiplication essentially collapsed to an equivalence, and it is presented as a positive example rather than an obstruction.
Used as an illustrative idempotent algebra in Pr^L; its tensor-square collapses back to spectra as a localization object.
Idempotent Sp⊗Sp≃Sp is a collapsed localization-type algebra used only as a positive illustration.
It is used as a standard illustration of idempotent algebra objects, and its idempotence is a localization/collapse property with Sp⊗Sp≃Sp.

examples.name: The unit map S -> MU
examples.note: The sphere-to-complex-cobordism unit is cited as evenly faithfully flat even though the induced map on all homotopy groups is not faithfully flat, illustrating the nonclassical meaning of the definition.
examples.engagement_reason: The unit map from the sphere to MU is cited as an example of an evenly faithfully flat map, with only a stated observation about evenness and no demonstrated verification or computation.

passes.reason: The unique unit map from the initial sphere spectrum is used to show evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat.
The unit map from the initial sphere ring spectrum to MU is used to show evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat.
The canonical unit map from the sphere spectrum to MU is a minimal/base-source map and is invoked to show evenly faithfully flat need not be ordinary faithfully flat.
The unit map from the initial sphere E∞-ring to MU is a minimal-base morphism used to show evenly faithfully flat need not be faithfully flat on homotopy.
The unit map starts from the initial E∞-ring S and is used to show evenly faithfully flat need not be ordinary π*-faithfully flat.
The canonical map from the initial/unit sphere spectrum to MU is used to show that evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat on homotopy groups.
Used to show evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat; the map is the canonical unit map from the initial sphere E∞-ring.
The map from the initial sphere ring to MU is used to show evenly faithful flatness need not be ordinary faithful flatness.
It is used to show that evenly faithfully flat maps need not be faithfully flat on homotopy, and the map starts from the initial/unit E∞ ring spectrum.
the canonical unit-source map S→MU has the minimal tensor-unit domain and is used to show that evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat on homotopy
The unit map S -> MU is used to show that evenly faithfully flat need not imply ordinary faithful flatness on homotopy, and its source is the sphere/unit base object.
It is cited to show evenly faithfully flat need not mean faithfully flat on homotopy, and its source is the initial/unit sphere spectrum.

examples.name: Ordinary rings as MU-algebras
examples.note: Ordinary rings are mentioned as a familiar class of MU-algebras to which the MU-relative evenly faithfully flat descent result can apply.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the THH(MU)->MU base-change argument to MU-algebras, including ordinary rings, identifies the resulting co-base-change as relative THH over MU, and explains consequences for the even filtration.

passes.reason: Ordinary rings are discrete or 0-truncated examples among spectral MU-algebras, used as routine instances.
Ordinary rings viewed as MU-algebras are discrete Eilenberg–Mac Lane ring spectra, a 0-truncated special case used for application rather than refutation.
Ordinary rings viewed as MU-algebras are discrete/Eilenberg–Mac Lane examples with no higher homotopy, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
Ordinary rings viewed as MU-algebras are discrete/Eilenberg–Mac Lane spectral algebras with no higher homotopy, used merely as examples.
Ordinary rings viewed as MU-algebras are discrete Eilenberg–Mac Lane objects with homotopy only in degree 0, used as routine examples.
Ordinary rings, viewed as MU-algebras via Eilenberg–Mac Lane spectra, are discrete/0-truncated compared with general spectral MU-algebras, and are used as routine examples.
Used as a routine source of MU-algebras; ordinary rings are discrete/0-truncated compared with general spectral MU-algebras.
Ordinary rings are discrete 0-truncated MU-algebras used as routine examples.
They are used as routine examples of MU-algebras, and as spectral MU-algebras ordinary rings are discrete/0-truncated.
ordinary rings viewed as MU-algebras are discrete Eilenberg–Mac Lane cases with higher homotopy collapsed, used as a standard application class
Ordinary rings are only cited as a familiar subclass of MU-algebras, and as Eilenberg-Mac Lane spectra they are discrete/0-truncated compared with general ring spectra.
Ordinary rings enter as discrete Eilenberg–Mac Lane MU-algebras, lacking the higher homotopy of typical spectral algebras.

examples.name: Algebraically closed commutative ring spectra with non-even K-theory and THH
examples.note: The loop/Tate spectra from Thomas's talk are algebraically closed as commutative ring spectra but have non-even K-theory and THH, motivating the idea that the right algebraic closures may live instead in stable dualizable infinity categories.
examples.engagement_reason: Algebraically closed commutative ring spectra with non-even K-theory and THH are invoked at the end as a motivating fact from another talk, without any calculation or construction carried out on them.

passes.reason: Algebraically closed ring spectra have a closure/maximality property but are cited because their K-theory and THH violate the desired evenness.
Algebraically closed commutative ring spectra are closure-maximal objects, and their non-even K-theory and THH are invoked against the hoped-for evenness/adequacy of that notion.
These algebraically closed commutative ring spectra are cited because their K-theory and THH are not even, obstructing the hoped-for behavior of algebraic closures in commutative ring spectra.
Algebraically closed ring spectra are closed/maximal in the algebraic-extension sense and are invoked to show algebraic closedness in spectra does not force even K-theory or THH.
Algebraically closed ring spectra are maximal/closed objects, and their non-even K-theory and THH are cited as an obstruction to this being the desired algebraic closure setting.

Algebraically closed ring spectra are maximal with respect to algebraic/étale extensions, and their non-even K-theory and THH are cited as an obstruction to the hoped-for even algebraic-closure behavior in commutative ring spectra.
Invoked as an obstruction to expecting algebraic closedness to give even K-theory or THH; algebraically closed objects are maximal with respect to algebraic extensions.
